title: Effect of cosolvent on the volume of activation for base hydrolysis of tris(1.10-phenanthroline)- and tris (2.2?-bipyridine)iron(II) complexes
abstract:
Effect of Cosolvent on the
Volume of Activation for
Base Hydrolysis of tris(1.10-
phenanthroline)- and tris
(2.2-bipyridine)Iron(II)
Complexes
JOHN BURGESS, COLIN D. HUBBARD1
Department of Chemistry, University of Leicester, Leicester, LE1 7RH, England
Received 28 June 1999; accepted 12 January 2000
ABSTRACT: The kinetics of the base hydrolysis of Fe(phen)3
2 and Fe(bipy)3
2 (phen  1,10-
phenanthroline and bipy  2,2’-bipyridine) in some aqueous alcohol mixtures at ambient and
elevated pressures (up to 1kbar) have been monitored spectrophotometrically at 25.0C. For
a given pressure, the alcohol cosolvent increases the rate of reaction relative to the reaction
in a wholly aqueous medium. In all cases, increasing pressure causes rate retardation and
derived volumes of activation for the reactions in aqueous solvent mixtures vary between 15
and 25 cm3 mol1, indicating that solvation changes of a different magnitude occur upon
reaching the transition state from those occurring for the reactions in aqueous medium. Since
the reaction has been established earlier to be nucleophilic attack of the incoming hydroxide
ion, the volumes of activation signify marked increases in the loss of electrostricted solvent
from the vicinities of the hydroxide ion and the iron(II) complex ions.  2000 John Wiley & Sons,
Inc. Int J Chem Kinet 32: 263–270, 2000
INTRODUCTION
In earlier studies, we have reported that the volume of
activation for base hydrolysis of iron(II)-diimine and
a related iron(II) complex is markedly affected by the
presence of cosolvents [1,2]. In aqueous solution, the
values of V* for base hydrolysis of Fe(hxsb)2,
Fe(Me2bsb)3
2, Fe(4MeObsb)3
2, Fe(3Mebsb)3
2, and
Correspondence to: J. Burgess (jbx@le.ac.uk)
1 Present address: Institute for Inorganic Chemistry, Friedrich-
Alexander-University of Erlangen-Nu¨rnberg, Egerlandstrasse 1,
91058 Erlangen, Germany
 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Fe(gmi)3
2 (ligand abbreviations are explained in
Scheme I), are almost the same and the range is barely
outside of experimental error [3–7]. The moderately
large positive values of V* (11 to 16 cm3 mol1)
have been interpreted as showing the importance of a
contribution from desolvation of the attacking nucle-
ophile (OH) and possibly of the metal complex ion
over the intrinsic volume change contribution, which
is negative and expected to be about 10 cm3 mol1
for an associative reaction [8]. An intrinsic volume
change will arise from stretching the Fe-N bonds in
the respective complexes in forming the transition
state. The extension has been calculated to be from
 264
BURGESS AND HUBBARD
1.97 A˚ to 2.6 A˚ for transition state formation for dis-
sociation of Fe(phen)3
2 [9]. Such an extension is
much greater than the difference between Fe-N bond
distances (established by X-ray diffraction methods)
in low-spin (Fe-N between 1.90 and 1.99 A˚ ) and anal-
ogous high-spin (Fe-N between 2.01 and 2.24 A˚ ) com-
plexes [10,11]. The fact that in the dissociation of
Fe(phen)3
2 it has been demonstrated that the forma-
tion of a one-bonded (leaving group) ligand species is
prevented by a steric conﬂict between the leaving lig-
and and C-H groups in the nonleaving ligands means
that the formation of a one-bonded intermediate of the
form Fe(phen)2(phen)(OH), where (phen) is a lig-
and bonded through only one N atom, is extremely
unlikely. The overall volume change for spin-state
crossovers is in the region of 3 to 15 cm3 mol1, with
more examples in the higher end of this range, for
several coordination complexes and heme derivatives
[12,13].
Base hydrolysis is clearly a different process from
dissociation or an equilibrium spin-state change, but
factors involved in the last two processes are signiﬁ-
cant in considering volume changes in base hydrolysis
of the title complexes. However, since a monodentate/
OH containing intermediate seems most implausible,
we shall use a one-step SN2 mechanism in interpreta-
tion of our results. The bond stretching, in the com-
plex, required to generate the transition state may well
vary with solvent, but there must be a signiﬁcant con-
tribution from the desolvation of OH and the latter
will vary markedly with solvent. The small range of
V* is strongly suggestive of an essentially common
contribution from hydroxide ion desolvation, although
variation in that quantity with a variable but coinci-
dentally compensating contribution from desolvation/
solvation of the metal complex ion cannot be ruled out
completely. Upon addition of mono-ols or, in some
cases, DMSO to the reaction solutions, reaction rates
are accelerated and vary signiﬁcantly and interesting
differences in the V* values were found [2,7]. The
reactions of complex ions possessing a hydrophobic
exterior were characterized by a signiﬁcant increase in
V*, an increase which was exhibited at lower mole
fractions of cosolvent with increasing bulk of the or-
ganic moiety of the alcohol cosolvent. It may be ar-
gued that this arises from the complex ion losing sol-
vating mono-ol upon formation of the transition state.
Conversely, the cations with a much less hydrophobic
periphery reacted in the presence of mono-ols to yield
much smaller positive values of V* and in some
cases small negative values. The combination of a
mixed solvent and a more hydrophilic cation exterior
could result in a different extent (less) of hydroxide
desolvation, or increasing solvation of the complex ion
by a mono-ol upon complete or partial charge neu-
tralization as the transition state is reached. However,
these suggestions are far from ﬁrm, and clearly, so far
no quantitative explanation of these results has been
realized. An obvious follow-up investigation in an at-
tempt to establish whether or not these ﬁndings are
general would involve determining the V* values for
base hydrolysis of the familiar tris-1.10-phenanthro-
line and tris-2.2-bipyridine complexes of iron(II) in
comparable media. As for the reactions of the other
complexes, the rate laws for base hydrolysis have been
established as second [14–17], providing the hydrox-
ide ion concentration, although still in considerable
excess over the complex ion concentration, is less than
those concentrations where second or higher terms in
hydroxide ion concentration complicate the analysis
of the experimental kinetics. Values of V* for the
base hydrolysis of Fe(phen)3
2 and Fe(bipy)3
2 in
aqueous solution have been reported previously as has
that for aquation of Fe(5Brphen)3
2 [4,18]. In order to
compile a set of results for comparative purposes, we
have monitored the kinetics of base hydrolysis of

EFFECT OF COSOLVENT ON THE VOLUME OF ACTIVATION FOR BASE HYDROLYSIS OF COMPLEXES
both in aqueous solution as well as in aqueous-cosol-
vent mixtures. The application of high-pressure kinet-
ics in mixed solvents as a method of examining the
role of solvation in reactivity and reactivity differ-
ences, the focus of this article, has sometimes been
combined with an approach that is a thermodynamic
one in which the inﬂuence of solvation is pursued by
analyzing transfer chemical potentials [19–21].
The perchlorate salts of Fe(phen)3
were prepared by established methods; absorption
spectra and molar absorptivities were consistent with
literature data. Sodium hydroxide solutions were made
from reagent-grade sodium hydroxide and distilled
water. The alcohols employed were reagent-grade ma-
Aqueous solutions and solutions of mixed solvents
were made immediately prior to use in kinetics exper-
iments. Aqueous solvent mixtures were made in vol-
ume percent before mixing. For kinetics experiments,
the reaction solution, immediately following prepara-
tion, was separated into two components, one for
measuring the kinetics at ambient pressure in 1 cm
cuvettes in the thermostated cell holder (25.0  0.1C)
of a Unicam SP 800 spectrophotometer, and the sec-
ond portion for introduction into the thermostatted
(25.0  0.1C), pressurizable container of the appa-
ratus described in detail in earlier literature [22].
2 [23] and 522 nm for Fe(bipy)3
absorbance of aliquots of solution withdrawn at suit-
able intervals from the high-pressure container was
measured immediately after withdrawal. All reactions
yielded very good ﬁrst-order kinetics over at least
three half-lives. Subsequently, the observed ﬁrst-order
rate constants at ambient pressure will be referred to
as k0 and those at pressures as kp. Volumes of activa-
tion were determined from slopes of plots of ln(kp/k0)
versus pressure. There was no indication that such
plots are nonlinear, and hence V* is assumed pres-
sure-independent over the range of pressure employed
and refers to the value at 0.1 MPa, with an estimated
error of 10%–15%. Mono-ol cosolvents accelerate
base hydrolysis compared with reaction in aqueous so-
lution. Therefore, lower hydroxide concentrations
were chosen for the more labile systems so as to render
the kinetics conveniently measureable and allow for
sufﬁcient time for thermal equilibration to occur fol-
The rate laws for base hydrolysis of Fe(phen)3
2 have been established, as referred to in the
Intoduction. Concentrations of sodium hydroxide used
in this study were such that the reactions are second-
order, ﬁrst-order in each reactant. There is no evidence
that these rate laws are not preserved when mono-ols
are added as cosolvents. There are two possible inter-
pretations, one which may be termed a bimolecular
substitution reaction mechanism (SN2) or a two-stage
reaction, but the latter alternative would involve a
long-lived hydroxide-containing intermediate of the
type already ruled out in the Introduction. The reaction
products per mole of initial complex ion are three
moles of 1,10-phenanthroline or 2,2-bipyridine and
one mole of aquated Fe2. Except in the case of the
rigorous exclusion of oxygen, the latter ultimately is
transformed to ferric hydroxide. The initial concentra-
tion of the complex ion was 0.075 mM, and the hy-
droxide ion concentrations were in the range from 0.67
mM to 2.0 mM. Measurements were made at pressures
up to 15,000 psi (ca. 104 MPa), and the results are
presented as kp/k0 values in Table I. The volumes of
activation derived from these data are also presented
in Table I. Selections of values for related systems
indicated in the Introduction are presented in Table II
(methanol cosolvent) and Table III (iso-propyl alcohol
and t-butyl alcohol cosolvents). In the pressure range
applied here there is usually no detectable pressure
dependence of the volume of activation [25,26]. In this
work the ln(kp/k0) versus p plots show some scatter but
no systematic evidence of curvature. Thus, the values
of V# reported refer to P  0.1 MPa.
A consideration of the trends in V# with different
mono-ols and different percentages of cosolvent may
be made based on the results presented in Tables I–
III. The cosolvents are methanol, iso-propyl alcohol,
We have shown previously [2,7] for base hydrolysis
of other similar iron(II) complexes that changes in V#
values are not very signiﬁcant in aqueous methanol
mixtures until a high proportion (50%) of methanol
is present, while the opposite is the case for t-butyl
Kinetics of Base Hydrolysis of Fe(phen)3
2 in Aqueous Mono-ol Mixtures at Elevated
Pressures and Derived Volumes of Activation, at 25.0C
In some cases the rate constants represent averages of more than one kinetic run.
Volumes of activation are reported to two signiﬁcant ﬁgures: values are subject to 10%–15% precision.
In wholly aqueous medium, values of 14 and 13 cm3 mol1 for Fe(phen)3
2, respectively, have been reported [4]. Higher
values were obtained using higher concentrations (0.10 mol dm3) of NaOH at 20.0C [18]. These differences have been addressed in an
Volumes of Activation for Base Hydrolysis of Several Iron(II) Complexes in
Aqueous or Aqueous Methanol Solutions at 25.0Ca
